title: Managing Constipation: Bowel Elimination and Comfort
subtitle: A 70-year-old man on around-the-clock opioids for pain, with low mobility, has not had a bowel movement in 3 days. The nurse manages constipation with fluids, fiber, activity, a toileting routine, and an appropriate bowel regimen.

patient.name: Constipation Management
patient.admitDate: 70M on scheduled opioids for pain, limited mobility, low fluid/fiber intake. No bowel movement in 3 days; reports bloating and straining.

vitals.temp: Opioids slow the bowel → start a regimen
Upright, footstool, privacy, respond to urge
Distension + vomiting + no stool = assess impaction/obstruction

labs.r: Not alone for opioids; risk if dehydrated.

nursesNote: A 70-year-old man on scheduled opioids for pain, with limited mobility and low fluid and fiber intake, has not had a bowel movement in 3 days and reports bloating and straining. Constipation means hard, infrequent, or difficult-to-pass stools; a daily bowel movement is not required for everyone. Opioids are a leading cause, so a bowel regimen should accompany opioid therapy. The nurse promotes first-line measures — increased fluids, dietary fiber, activity, privacy, an upright position with a footstool, and responding promptly to the urge — and gives ordered laxatives, favoring a stimulant (senna) with or without an osmotic agent (polyethylene glycol) for opioid-induced constipation; bulk-forming agents alone are not effective for opioids and require adequate fluid. New abdominal distension with no stool, nausea, and vomiting suggests impaction or obstruction and must be evaluated before further laxatives.
providerOrders: Assess bowel pattern, last BM, bowel sounds, and abdomen
Encourage fluids, dietary fiber, activity, and a toileting routine
Start a prophylactic bowel regimen with opioid therapy
Administer ordered stimulant/osmotic laxatives as indicated
Evaluate for impaction/obstruction if distension and vomiting occur

opts.text: Opioid analgesics commonly cause constipation
Immobility and low fluid/fiber intake increase the risk
Constipation means hard, infrequent, or difficult-to-pass stools
Everyone must have a bowel movement every day to be normal
Ignoring the urge to defecate can contribute to constipation
Increasing activity has no effect on bowel function
Fluids, fiber, and activity are first-line for most patients
A prophylactic bowel regimen should start when opioids are started
Bulk-forming laxatives need adequate fluid intake to work safely
A stimulant laxative (senna) is appropriate for opioid-induced constipation
Bulk-forming agents alone reliably treat opioid-induced constipation
No stool with distension, nausea, and vomiting needs evaluation for impaction/obstruction

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the approach to a constipated patient

opts.text: Assess the patient's normal bowel pattern and current symptoms
Check for red flags (distension, vomiting, no flatus) of obstruction
Encourage fluids, fiber, activity, and a toileting routine
Administer ordered osmotic/stimulant laxatives as indicated
Reassess for a bowel movement and continue a prevention plan
Increasing dietary fiber and fluids
Encouraging ambulation and activity
Starting a bowel regimen along with opioid therapy
Providing privacy and an upright position with a footstool
A stimulant laxative for opioid-induced constipation
Giving a bulk-forming laxative without adequate fluids
Relying on daily enemas for routine management
Encouraging the patient to ignore the urge until convenient

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Sequence care for no BM in 3 days on opioids
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess bowel sounds, distension, last BM, and check for impaction
Confirm no signs of obstruction before giving laxatives
Give the ordered osmotic and/or stimulant laxative
Encourage fluids, fiber, activity, and a toileting routine
Reassess for results, document, and continue a prophylactic regimen
"I'll drink more fluids and eat more fruits, vegetables, and whole grains."
"Walking and staying active can help my bowels move."
"Since I take an opioid, I'll use the bowel regimen to prevent constipation."
"I should hold it and ignore the urge when I'm busy."
"A daily enema is the best way to stay regular."
"I'll take my fiber supplement with a full glass of water."
"No stool plus a swollen belly and vomiting is fine to ignore."
"I don't have to have a bowel movement every single day to be healthy."

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Basic Care and Comfort, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
